external_references.source: Creative Commons 'What's new in 4.0' (patents and trademarks)
external_references.summary: Creative Commons documents that CC 2.0 (like 3.0) was silent on whether patent and trademark rights were being licensed. CC 4.0 added Section 2(b)(2) to make the non-license of patent and trademark rights explicit; CC 2.0 has no equivalent clause, relying only on the generic reservation in §3 ('All rights not expressly granted ... are hereby reserved').

permissions.commentary: CC-BY-SA-2.0 is silent on patent rights. There is no clause equivalent to CC 4.0 Section 2(b)(2) ('Patent and trademark rights are not licensed under this Public License'). The only hook is the closing sentence of Section 3 (s-61), which reserves 'all rights not expressly granted.' The practical reading is that no patent rights are granted, but because the text does not say so, this is best marked grey with commentary.

external_references.source: Creative Commons 'What's new in 4.0' (patents and trademarks)
external_references.summary: Creative Commons notes that CC 2.0 and 3.0 are silent on the licensor's own trademark rights. The only trademark language in CC 2.0 concerns the 'Creative Commons' trademark itself. CC 4.0 made the non-license of the licensor's trademark rights explicit (Section 2(b)(2)).

limitations.commentary: Unlike CC-BY-SA-4.0, the 2.0 text is silent on the licensor's trademark rights. The only explicit trademark language concerns Creative Commons' own mark (s-118 to s-119) and restricts the parties from using the 'Creative Commons' trademark. There is no analogue to CC 4.0 §2(b)(2) stating that 'trademark rights are not licensed.'
